coredata.dc:title: Single-step synthesis of self-assembled carbon dots for enhanced cancer cell retention and theranostics applications
coredata.dc:creator: Reddy Padidam, Sindeesh
Balasaheb Kadam, Devyani
Thakkellapati, Saichand
Verma, Mansi
Raichur, Ashok M.
Narashimhan Ramana, Lakshmi
coredata.dc:description: Photothermal therapy is a promising strategy for killing cancer cells, yet the use of carbon dots as a photothermal agent has limited its use due to the high excretion rate from the cancer cell due to its smaller size of less than 10 nm. In this study, we have developed a strategy for synthesizing self-assembled carbon dots with higher photothermal and lesser excretion from cells. Single-step synthesis of self-assembled carbon dots using thermal degradation method process using lecithin as carbon source. The self-assembly of lecithin-derived carbon dots is induced in the presence of water molecules. Excitingly, lecithin-derived self-assembly of carbon dots (LDSCD) shows superior photothermal efficacy even at low concentrations and fluorescence is red-shifted. Moreover, the LDSCD exhibits higher phototoxicity on incubation with cancer cells for two hours followed by near-infrared light exposure after twenty-four hours. The confocal images of the cells incubated with lecithin-derived self-assembly of carbon dots at two-time intervals (4 and 24 hrs) demonstrated higher retention of self-assembled quantum dots compared to the smaller-size carbon dots (<50 nm).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photothermal
Lecithin-derived self-assembled carbon dots
Near-infrared light
Cytotoxicity
Photocytotoxicity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of lecithin-derived self-assembled derived quantum dots

Cellular uptake and retention of lecithin-derived self-assembled derived quantum dots
Invitro cytotoxicity and phototoxicity of lecithin-derived self-assembled derived quantum dots
Physiochemical characterisation of lecithin derived self-assemble carbon dots
Cellular uptake, cytotoxicity and phototoxicity of LDSCD

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) UV spectra B) TEM image C) Photoluminescence D) Photothermal temperature E) Photothermal temperature change of LDSCD.
A) XPS of LDSCD shows carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and phosphorous B) XPS of C 1 s C) XPS of N 1 s D) XPS of O 1 s E) XPS of P 2p.

Cellular internalisation of LDSCD in Hela cells using confocal microscopy A) Control Hela cells B) Hela cells incubated with LDSCD for 4 h and C) Hela cells treated with LDSCD for four hours and washed followed by incubation in culture medium for 24 h (Scalebar (white line): 10 µm).
A) Cytotoxicity of LDSCD incubated with Hela cells B) Photocytotoxicity of LDSCD incubated with Hela cells at 4 and 24 h.

head.ce:title: Single-step synthesis of self-assembled carbon dots for enhanced cancer cell retention and theranostics applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Manipal Institute of Regenerative Medicine (MIRM), Manipal Academy of Higher Education (MAHE), Allalasandra GKVK Post, Yelahanka, Bengaluru 560065, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Manipal Institute of Regenerative Medicine (MIRM), Manipal Academy of Higher Education (MAHE)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Manipal Institute of Regenerative Medicine (MIRM), Manipal Academy of Higher Education (MAHE), Allalasandra GKVK Post, Yelahanka, Bengaluru 560065, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biomedical Engineering Department, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX 77843, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biomedical Engineering Department, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX 77843, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Science, Bengaluru 560012, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Engineering, Indian Institute of Science, Bengaluru 560012, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Manipal institute of Regenerative medicine, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal, Bengaluru.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photothermal therapy is a promising strategy for killing cancer cells, yet the use of carbon dots as a photothermal agent has limited its use due to the high excretion rate from the cancer cell due to its smaller size of less than 10 nm. In this study, we have developed a strategy for synthesizing self-assembled carbon dots with higher photothermal and lesser excretion from cells. Single-step synthesis of self-assembled carbon dots using thermal degradation method process using lecithin as carbon source. The self-assembly of lecithin-derived carbon dots is induced in the presence of water molecules. Excitingly, lecithin-derived self-assembly of carbon dots (LDSCD) shows superior photothermal efficacy even at low concentrations and fluorescence is red-shifted. Moreover, the LDSCD exhibits higher phototoxicity on incubation with cancer cells for two hours followed by near-infrared light exposure after twenty-four hours. The confocal images of the cells incubated with lecithin-derived self-assembly of carbon dots at two-time intervals (4 and 24 hrs) demonstrated higher retention of self-assembled quantum dots compared to the smaller-size carbon dots (<50 nm).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer mortality rate has increased in the last decade due to the higher resistance of the tumor cells against conventional therapy [1]. Despite the development of modern technology still, there is a lack in the diagnosis and treatment of cancer. Currently, cancer treatment mainly includes surgery, chemotherapy, radiation, immunotherapy, targeted therapy, and combinational therapy of the above-mentioned [2]. However, the therapies led to the development of highly resistant varieties and side effects due to higher dosages of drugs or radiation thus limiting the therapeutic efficacy [3]. Alternate modern-day Nano therapeutics based on the nanocarrier loaded with anticancer drugs along with a targeted delivery system like folic acid or antibody-conjugated nanocarriers [4,5]. Despite the above strategies, stimuli-responsive Nano therapeutics like photodynamic, photothermal, sonodynamic, and chemodynamic therapies are the modern-day attraction [6]. The light-based therapies are photodynamic and photothermal therapies which utilised light energy in production of heat energy and free radical respectively which damages the targeted cells.

Photothermal agents are the effective and emerging non-invasive therapeutic strategy which utilises a light sensitive agent along with the higher wavelength light source mainly near infra-red. The advantage of photothermal therapy (Photothermal therapy) results in synergy effect by induced immunotherapy and production of short-lived reactive oxygen species (photodynamic therapy) [7,8]. The main photothermal agents are classified into two types mainly inorganic nanoparticles gold, silver, chalcogenides like molybdenum / tungsten sulphide derived nanoparticles and organic nanoparticles like graphene oxide, carbon dots [9,10]. The clinical limitation of the inorganic photothermal agents are highly toxic in nature, expensive and non-metabolised in the body [11,12].

Carbon derived nanodots are modern-day nanoparticles with a wide range of applications from energy materials to biomedical applications [13]. Carbon dots are zero-dimensional carbon allotrope with high biocompatible nature, bioimaging, highly excretable and metabolised [14,15]. The physicochemical properties of the carbon dots can be manipulated based on the biomedical applications. The limitation of the carbon dots in the biomedical application are faster cellular clearances and low photothermal efficacy [16,17]. The nanoparticles in the size range of less than 50 nm are easily excreted compared to the 100–200 nm [18]. Previously we have observed exocytosis of different charge carbon dots in melanoma cells. The cationic charge carbon dots are retained in cells compared to anionic charge [19]. Based on our previous experience we want to develop a larger size carbon dots with higher retention rate and photothermal conversion.

In the present manuscript, we have synthesised self-assembled carbon dots by single-step hydrothermal method using lecithin as the raw material. The lecithin derived self-assembled carbon dots were characterised for the physiochemical properties and their cellular uptake, retention and their photothermal therapeutic efficacy were studied and reported using cancer cell line. The LDSCD retention was also compared with the smaller size quantum dots (<50 nm) derived from dextrose and lecithin.
Soyabean lecithin (95 %) was purchased from Avanti polar lipids. MTT, Fetal calf serum, Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle medium was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Bangalore, India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of lecithin-derived self-assembled derived quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of LDSCD was carried out by Thermal degradation method using lecithin as the substrate. About 2 gm of lecithin was taken in a petri dish and was subjected to heating using microwave oven (600 W) for 30 min. The brown powder obtained is now added to water at 65 ⁰C which is in stirring condition and the molecules were allowed to self-assemble. The solution was left over for stirring at 800 rpm at 65 ⁰C for 2 h. The solution is further centrifuged at 5000 rpm to remove the unreacted lecithin molecules. The supernatant is taken and allowed to pass through the syringe filter of 200 nm pore size. The solution is further purified using dialysis to remove the smaller carbon dots of size less than 20 nm leaving behind the LDSCD inside the dialysis bag. The LDSCD solution is freeze dried for further characterisation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of lecithin-derived self-assembled derived quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The physicochemical characterisation of LDSCD was analysed as described below. The UV spectra of lecithin and LDSCD were read using Nanodrop ND-1000 UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The surface morphology and the distribution of smaller dots in the LDSCD was analysed using the Higher resolution Transmission Electron microscopy (FEI Technai F30, Netherland). The hydrodynamic radius and surface charge of LDSCD was determined using the zeta-sizer (Nano-ZS, Malvern, UK). The photoluminescence of LDSCD sample were recorded using the Perkin Elmer Luminescence spectrometer (LS-55, USA). The surface chemistry of LDSCD was analysed using KratoAxis ULTRA X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy under standard operating procedure. The photothermal temperature of the LDSCD dispersed in buffer was recorder using home-made photothermal camera (Seek thermal imager for Android, Model UV-AAA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and retention of lecithin-derived self-assembled derived quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The Cervical cancer cell line (Hela Cells, ATCC cell line, USA) was used as a model cell line for cellular uptake of LDSCD. The cells were culture in DMEM medium supplemented with fetal calf serum (10 %) and streptomycin (1 %). The cellular uptake and clearance of LDSCD (size > 50 nm), dextrose derived quantum dots (Size < 50 nm) and lecithin derived quantum dots (Size < 50 nm) was observed for two time points 4 and 24 h [20] . The dextrose derived quantum dots protocol was already optimised in our previous paper [19].

4 X 105 cells were seeded in cover slip and left for twenty-four hours for cell attachment in a 24 well plate. Further the cells were incubated with 10 µg/ml of LDSCD, dextrose derived quantum dots and lecithin derived quantum dots (<50 nm: Size) for four hours. Further the cells were washed with phosphate buffer saline to remove the excess sample and other set of well containing the cells with LDSCD was left for 24 h to check for the clearance of the quantum dots. The cover slip was treated with 4 % paraformaldehyde and imaged using confocal microscopy at excitation of 543 nm and emission at 567 nm for LDSCD, excitation of 405 and 488 nm for dextrose derived quantum dots, excitation of 495 and 525 nm for lecithin derived quantum dots (Zeiss LSM 710, Carl Zeiss Microimaging inc., Thornwood, USA).
The LDSCD incubated cells for 4 h along with the 24 h samples were further read for fluorescence intensity at excitation of 543 nm and emission of 567 nm with only cells as control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Invitro cytotoxicity and phototoxicity of lecithin-derived self-assembled derived quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Cytotoxicity and phototoxicity of LDSCD was determined using the MTT Assay (CellTiter96 Aqueous one solution, Promega, USA) as per standard protocol. Briefly 1 X 104 Hela cells were seeded in a 96 well plate incubated with different concentration (10, 20, 40, 80 and 100 µg/ml in buffer) of LDSCD for 4 h at 37 ⁰C. The cells were washed with PBS to remove the excess media and again refilled with 200 µl of fresh media for 24 h in the incubator. The cells were then treated with MTT reagent (200 µl) and incubated for two hours at 37 ⁰C. The colour developed were read at 490 nm using microplate reader for both treated and untreated samples. The data is represented as Average ± SD (n = 3).

The phototoxicity was conducted similar to cytotoxicity studies as shown above. The phototoxicity was evaluated at two time point four hours and twenty hours. Two set of 1 X 104 Hela cells were seeded in a 96 well plate incubated with different concentration (10, 20, 40, 80 and 100 µg/ml in buffer) of LDSCD for 4 h at 37 ⁰C. In one set, cells were washed with PBS and exposed to NIR laser (808 nm, 0.5 W cm−2) for six minutes followed by MTT assay. In another set of cells incubated with LDSCD for 4 h at 37 ⁰C, cells were washed with PBS and further filled with fresh media and incubated for 24 h. The cells were then exposed to NIR laser (808 nm, 0.5 W cm−2) for six minutes and MTT assay was performed. The data were recorded for 4 and 24 h at 490 nm using microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiochemical characterisation of lecithin derived self-assemble carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–visible spectra of lecithin dispersion and LDSCD dispersion were analysed (Fig. 1 A). The lecithin-derived self-assembled quantum dots show two peaks at 270 and 365 nm due to π → π* and n → π* transition which is similar to earlier reports. The π → π* and n → π* is mainly due to the aromatic sp2 domain and functional groups derived from phosphate, nitrogen, and oxygen attached to carbon moieties respectively [21,22].

The surface morphology of the LDSCD was imaged using Transmission electron microscopy (Fig. 1B). The TEM images display black cluster particles with tiny contrast dots forming a spherical aggregated morphology of lecithin-derived carbon dot dispersion in water. The average size of individual self-assembled carbon dots in the self-assembled cluster is around 3 ± 1 nm with a size range of 1 to 6 nm with a cluster size of 87 nm. The lecithin-derived self-assembled carbon dots shows black and grey region patterns in aggregates. The average lattice spacing of LDSCD was about 4 nm. The clustered spherical nature of self-assembled quantum dots assembled is driven by the amphipathic nature of the lecithin molecules when dispersed in water similar to the formation of the liposome [23].

DLS shows the hydrodynamic radius of the self-assembly carbon dot of 150 ± 50 nm. The hydrodynamic radius is slightly higher than the actual size of the lecithin-derived carbon dots due to the aggregation of the nanoparticles in the liquid medium [24]. The surface charge of the self-assembly carbon dot is around − 25.6 ± 2 mV. The negatively charged zeta potential is due to the presence of functional groups mainly the phosphate and hydroxy group present on the surface of the carbon dots determined by XPS [25,26].

The photoluminescence data of lecithin-derived self-assembled carbon dots are shown in the Fig. 1C. The samples exhibit excitation-dependent emission due to the presence of various functional groups which act as heterogenous emissive traps that provide radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs [27]. The intensity of emission in the blue and green regions is weaker compared to the red region due to the larger size of the lecithin-derived self-assembled carbon dots. The graphitic nitrogen (XPS data), electron rich phosphate group (XPS data), π conjugated sp2 carbon (Raman spectra) and larger size of the LDSCD results in red-shift thus resulting in higher intensity in the fluorescence spectra which is previously reported [28–30].

XPS analysis of LDSCD confirmed the presence of oxygen (O), nitrogen (N) and phosphorous (P) on the LDSCD surface (Fig. 2 A–E). The four prominent peaks at 284 eV, 399 eV, 532 eV and 133 eV has been ascribed to the carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and phosphorous, respectively (Fig. 2A). The deconvoluted C1s spectra (Fig. 2B) showcase four prominent peaks at 284, 285, 287 eV corresponding to the C = C, C-O and C = O respectively [31]. The deconvoluted N1s spectra (Fig. 2C) exhibit two prominent peaks at 401 and 399 eV corresponding to the amide nitrogen O = C-N or graphitic nitrogen and N-(C)3 or H-N-(C)2, respectively [32,33]. The deconvoluted peaks in the O1s spectra (Fig. 2D) at 530, 532 and 533 eV has been attributed to C-OH, C = O and C-O-C respectively [34,35]. The single peak observed in the P1s spectra (Fig. 2E) at ∼ 133 eV has been assigned to the P-O group [36].

Photothermal profiles of LDSCD was displayed in below Fig. 1D (concentration vs photothermal temperature). The photothermal temperature increases with the concentration of the LDSCD quantum dots (10–100 µg/ml) with NIR laser exposure time = of 6 min. An increase in temperature up to 54 ⁰C at a maximum concentration of 100 µg/ml from 37 ⁰C at a minimum concentration of 10 µg/ml. In case of the blank buffer the temperature increases to 28 ⁰C. The observed photothermal conversion is higher at low concentration due to the aggregated quantum dots resulting in higher adsorption of infrared light reported recently [37]. The temperature change (Δ ⁰C) in time point of 6 min is plotted vs concentration (Fig. 1E). It was observed that the temperature change increase with concentration and reaches a maximum of four to six folds at a concentration of 40 to 100 µg/ml compared to the control buffer illustrating the importance of the self-assembled molecules [38]. The photothermal properties of the LDSCD is mainly due to the oscillation of free electrons in the sp2 hybridised graphitic region (supplementary Fig. S1: Raman spectra showing g band) of the quantum dots [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake, cytotoxicity and phototoxicity of LDSCD
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake of LDSCD was evaluated using confocal microscopy (Fig. 3 ). The LDSCD shows higher wavelength red shift due to the increased size of the particle size up to 100 nm and also the electron rich phosphorous group results in red shifted [40]. The LDSCD is found mainly in the cytoplasm of the cells as aggregated form. Moreover, the cells retain normal cell integrity even with higher concentration of LDSCD. Moreover, the cellular uptake of the self-assembled LDSCD were incubated for 4 h and further the cells were washed and left for 24 h for checking the excretion of the quantum dots using confocal microscopy. It was observed that the quantum dots are distributed in the cytoplasm of the cells both 4 and 24 h and the intensity doesn’t significantly decrease as observe in the confocal microscopy. Further quantitative measurement of the fluorescence intensity for the above samples (using plate readers) exhibits little decrease in the intensity for 24 h samples compared to 4 h (Supplementary Fig. S2). The nanoparticles in the size range of 50 – 200 nm are easily endocytosed by the cells with higher retention compared to smaller nanoparticles of less than 50 nm [41,42]. The nanoparticles less than 50 nm are easily excreted from the cell which is demonstrated using dextrose derived and lecithin derived quantum dots which shows decrease fluorescence intensity with longer incubation time (Supplementary Fig. S3 and S4).

The Cytotoxicity cell viability was checked with different concentrations of LDSCD using MTT reagent as shown in Fig. 4 . The LDSCD shows good biocompatibility even at a higher concentration of 100 µg/ml with only 5 % death. The phototoxicity of LDSCD was evaluated in presence of carbon dots at various concentration of 10 to 100 µg/ml and exposed to NIR light for 6 min. The phototoxicity shows dose dependent in a linear fashion with prominent cell death at concentration of 40 µg/ml with 50 % of cell death. Nearly 80 % cell death at high concentration of 100 µg/ml due to the increased cellular uptake of LDSCD and also higher photothermal temperature causing the cell death. The control cells exposed with NIR light didn’t exhibit any toxicity. The phototoxicity was evaluated in two time points 4 and 24 h to evaluate the excretion of LDSCD. The phototoxicity data at both times points (4 and 24) illustrates there is no significant difference between the toxicity illustrating the LDSCD remains intact within the cells without excretion.

In summary, lecithin derived self-assembled derived carbon dots are prepared by simple one step hydrothermal method using lecithin as a source of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and phosphorous without external doping. The self-assembly was initiated in the presence of water and further the sample size is optimised by passing through the filter membrane to obtain the uniform sized nanoparticles. The fluorescence of LDSCD is red shifted due to the increased in size and phosphorous doping. The cellular uptake and phototoxicity exhibited at two times point four and twenty-four hours in cancer cells illustrated low clearance, higher retention and higher phototoxicity demonstrating the superior theranostics application of the LDSCD.

Sindeesh Reddy Padidam: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology. Devyani Balasaheb Kadam: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Saichand Thakkellapati: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Mansi Verma: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Ashok M. Raichur: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Lakshmi Narashimhan Ramana: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110144.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
